cause: the run-start leash recorded the schema change as 'purely additive ... so every frozen layout scenario pinning the single-tree form stays true and no Clearance floor fires'. Reading the frozen suite falsified that premise: Clearance FIRED.
detail: layout.feature's frozen 'Scenario Outline: exactly one of root and panes' carries the Examples row 'neither root nor panes -> exits 1'. A tabs-only template declares neither root nor panes, so the frozen contract REFUSES the exact thing this CR exists to add. The outline had to be rewritten to the three-form schema, which is a re-open and a narrowing however the net reads: the row's replacement ('none of root, panes or tabs') no longer refuses a tabs-only template. The leash line was written at run start before the suite was read, so it was a prediction, not a claim of record - but it was wrong and would have been carried into the gate why as an unexamined 'no floor fired'. Pre-authorized by the requester at intake once the conflict was surfaced, with the cheaper alternative (overload root to accept a tab list, dodging the rewrite entirely) offered and declined. Caught in explore by reading the frozen scenario rather than trusting the leash; the cold spec-judge independently flagged the same stale ledger claim, confirming it was not self-serving to leave uncorrected.
fix: the gate why.floor states the Clearance fired and is pre-authorized, rather than repeating the leash's 'no floor' prediction. The leash line stands as written - the ledger is append-only and a wrong prediction corrected in place would erase the fact that it was made.

cause: the spec specified a consequence and omitted its mechanism. layout.feature's frozen "herdr's root tab is named after birth" requires naming a space AFTER birth, and mux/README states the cost as 'one tab rename on herdr's first tab' - but mux.feature's 5 new scenarios added only the group id, and SessionAdapter has no relabel member at all. The spec gate passed this: the cold judge checked pairwise consistency and the miss test, neither of which surfaces a mechanism that no scenario names.
detail: Caught by the impl-producer, which stopped and reported rather than inventing a seam member in a file it did not own - the correct call, and the reason the gap is a correction rather than a shipped defect. Two facts made it load-bearing rather than cosmetic. First, SessionAdapter has no rename: herdr's 'pane rename' exists only INSIDE open, private to the pane:* branch and unreachable for a tab or workspace, and tmux has no rename-window in src/ at all. Second, the only truthful has-a-workspace-tier signal is OpenedPane.workspace, which is absent on tmux by design and present on herdr on every route - but it does not exist until the first open RETURNS, so tmux cannot name its window at birth either. Both label branches therefore need a post-birth rename, which inverts the walk's shape from name-at-birth to open-then-rename. Building 10 scenarios against a structure that may invert is what stopping avoided.
fix: add a relabel member to the seam plus additive mux.feature scenarios for it. Additive to a frozen suite, so it SELF-CLEARS - no re-open, no Clearance, and the freeze holds. Rejected the alternative of a declared hasWorkspaceTier flag (the canSizeSplits precedent): it does not stand alone, since herdr's root tab needs the rename regardless, so it would buy only the tmux name-at-birth case at the price of a second capability declaration. OpenedPane.workspace already means exactly 'this backend has a workspace tier' and is already specified as absent-rather-than-false, so reading the tier off it invents no new vocabulary.

cause: second gap of the same shape as seq:2 - the spec named a behavior the seam cannot reach. rename(target, 'tab', name) addresses a TAB id, but OpenedPane reports only { id: <pane>, workspace? }. The walk has no tab id for the workspace's root tab, so it cannot name it, which is exactly what layout.feature's 'herdr's root tab is named after birth' requires.
detail: The trap is that a naive fix passes its tests. Established live rather than reasoned: 'herdr tab rename w4S:p1 x' returns tab_not_found and exits 1, while 'tmux rename-window -t %1' RESOLVES a pane id and succeeds (3.6b). Since the adapter discards a failed command's output, passing a pane id would leave herdr's root tab named '1' while every mocked test stayed green - a false bind on the worst possible split, green on one backend and silently broken on the other. The impl-producer stopped and left 4 scenarios unbound rather than half-ship it, and verified the failure against both binaries instead of assuming. Root cause, common to seq:2 and seq:3: the layout behavior was specified without walking the seam's actual return shape and verb list, so both gaps are the seam lacking a fact or a verb the walk needs. The spec gate could not catch either - a cold judge checks pairwise consistency and the miss test, and neither surfaces a mechanism no scenario names.
fix: widen OpenedPane with an optional tab, additive to the frozen mux.feature so the freeze self-clears. Costs nothing and invents nothing: both backends ALREADY emit it in the output the pane id is read from (herdr's create envelope carries result.root_pane.tab_id, confirmed live as w4S:t1 alongside result.tab.label === '1', which independently re-proves the root-tab constraint; tmux's grouping open already reads #{window_id}). This is the same move, and the same argument, as CR 9's workspace widening - a surface that hid it would be discarding a fact it already held. Rejected the alternative of rename('tab') taking a pane id and each adapter resolving the tab: it costs herdr an extra pane get and makes the target's meaning tier-dependent.

cause: a test titled with a frozen scenario name SURVIVED the mutation of its own subject - the false bind this project's binding discipline exists to prevent, caught by the impl-producer running the mutation rather than reading the test.
detail: 'a tabs template still defaults --at to workspace' was written against tmux. tmux collapses the workspace and tab tiers onto the same new-window, so the identical argv is emitted at EITHER placement and no assertion over it can tell the two apart: mutating the default from 'workspace' to 'tab' left the test green. The test claimed its scenario and verified nothing. Rewritten on herdr, where the tiers are genuinely distinct verbs (workspace create vs tab create); the mutation now kills it. Worth recording as a general hazard rather than a one-off: tmux is the WRONG backend to bind any scenario that turns on the workspace/tab distinction, because that distinction does not exist there. Any such scenario must be bound on herdr.
fix: test moved to herdr and re-proven by mutation.

cause: the conductor reported a self-imposed design limit to the requester as if it were a fact about the backends, and proposed scoping the spec's prose down to match it. The requester rejected the premise - 'you have to introduce and define @cm_ws anyway, so I can't see why that is not possible' - and was right.
detail: The claim was that 'worktree add --layout' cannot group its windows on tmux because the route's first region is opened before the walk runs. True of the seam AS WRITTEN, and false as a constraint: @cm_ws is an ordinary tmux window option settable on any window at any time, and the worktree open already returns OpenedPane.tab, which on tmux IS the window id. Everything needed to tag it is in hand the moment the open returns. The actual blocker was that the group id was specified as an OPEN OPTION ONLY, so no verb can tag a space that already exists - a choice the conductor made, then relayed as a discovered limitation. The proposed live probe of herdr's worktree verbs was doubly wrong: nothing needs passing at the worktree open, and herdr ignores the tag regardless. Cost of the real fix is near zero, since tmux's set-option is ALREADY a second call after new-window - there is no birth flag for it - so routing it through a verb adds no call.
fix: a group(exec, target, {id, name}) seam verb acting on an already-open space, the same shape as rename; open's workspaceGroup routes through it internally, exactly as open's pane label already routes through rename. Additive, so the freeze self-clears.

cause: on tmux, re-applying a captured workspace DOUBLE-PREFIXES every tab label, so the frozen scenario 're-applying a captured workspace reproduces the tabs it was captured from' is FALSE on that backend. The suite does not catch it: the round-trip test binds in layout-capture.test.ts over pure derived structures with no backend, and 'a captured tab keeps the label its tab carries' binds on herdr, which has a real tier and never prefixes. Green everywhere, broken on tmux.
detail: apply names the window 'pool - editor'; capture reads the window name back as the tab's label; re-apply prefixes again, yielding 'pool - pool - editor'. Surfaced by the capture impl-producer, which reported it as 'the contract's chosen trade' rather than a defect - an honest report, but the wrong classification: a frozen scenario is contradicted on one backend, which is a defect, not a trade. Root cause is the conductor's, and it is the same error as seq:5 in a second place: the spec establishes 'the label is a human's to read, an opaque option carries what a machine reads' and then applies it to the WORKSPACE ONLY. The tab's own name has the identical property - once the display name is composed, the true name is unrecoverable without parsing the very separator proven ambiguous. tmux has one name field per window, so the composed name destroys the original; herdr is unaffected, since its tab label is the true name and is never composed.
fix: tmux stores the tab's true name in a second window option (@cm_tab) beside the group id, written by the same group verb; describeWorkspace reports the label from it rather than from the composed window name, falling back to the window name where no tag exists. No label is parsed anywhere, the round-trip holds on both backends, and the human-readable prefix the requester chose is kept. Additive.

cause: third instance of ONE pattern, and the pattern is the finding: a scenario stated ROUTE-AGNOSTICALLY, implemented on one route, bound to a test on that route, and silently false on the other. Each instance was green in CI and wrong on a real backend.
detail: The scenario 'on a backend with no workspace tier, a tab is labeled with its workspace and its own name' says 'When it is applied on tmux' - naming no route, so it covers open --layout AND worktree add --layout. applyTabsToRegion never renamed its first tab, so that route left the window named 'pool' instead of 'pool - editor'; the herdr sibling is equally unmet. Invisible because the test binds on the open --layout route only. The two earlier instances are the same shape: the walk grouped only on the route that opened the region (seq:5), and capture took the composed display name on the route where the name is composed (seq:6). All three trace to one conductor habit: specifying a behavior against the route being built at the time, while wording it as a property of the template. A template cannot mean two things depending on which verb reached it, and the suite cannot catch that when each scenario is bound on exactly one route. The impl-producer reported this one as an observation with 'no frozen scenario asks' - an honest read that came from the test's binding rather than from the scenario's own text, which is precisely how the pattern hides.
fix: share the first-tab naming between both routes rather than implementing it twice - the shared path is what prevents instance four - and bind BOTH routes to the same route-agnostic scenario keys (many-to-one binding is legal; the fold passes only if none fail). A route-agnostic scenario deserves route-agnostic verification. Standing lesson for this node: when a scenario names no route, verify every route, because binding one and passing is not evidence about the other.

cause: label uniqueness was an invented rule, and the conductor invented it TWICE more before the requester dismantled the premise. It refused a duplicate tab label and a cross-tab duplicate pane label 'because labels are the manifest's keys' - copying CR 5's conclusion without checking whether its premise held one tier up. It does not: the manifest reports a pane's tab by INDEX, not by label, so nothing keys on a tab's name at all.
detail: Probed rather than argued, once the requester asked 'does tmux reject it?'. It does not: three windows named logs, exit 0, each still addressable by its own window id; three panes titled worker, exit 0. herdr's api schema has ZERO mentions of unique/duplicate/conflict/taken, and PaneRenameParams requires only pane_id. Decisively, the requester's own live herdr server held TEN tabs labelled '1' - herdr MANUFACTURES duplicates, labelling every new workspace's root tab 1, which is the same constraint this CR had already documented as the reason herdr's root tab needs a rename. The conductor had that fact in hand the whole time and failed to draw the conclusion from it. The conductor then defended CR 5's PANE rule as justified-by-the-manifest-lookup-promise and correctly noted the ratio precedent (the schema legitimately imposes bars the seam does not - the seam renders ratio 0 while the schema refuses it). The requester dismantled that too, with the argument that decides it: a user renames panes and tabs BY HAND, and layout save exists to capture exactly that, so the uniqueness rule forced capture to DROP a shared label from both panes - discarding the very fact the capture exists to preserve, and reporting 'no label' where there is one, against this node's own absent-rather-than-false convention. CR 8's own scenario comment had already flagged the friction ('the one place the live model and the schema genuinely disagree') and resolved it by making capture lossy; the requester resolved it the other way, by making the schema match the live model. A pool of three panes all named worker is a legitimate thing to mean.
fix: Clearance, pre-authorized by the requester: 3 scenarios REMOVED (CR 5's 'a duplicate label is a validation error because labels are manifest keys'; CR 8's 'a label two panes share is dropped from both'; and the earlier-cleared outline) and 1 MODIFIED ('every validation error is reported at once' - its Given used a duplicate label as one of three errors, so the third became an absolute dir; the scenario's actual point, all errors at once each with a JSON path, is untouched). The conductor's own two invented rules simply stop being added. Replaced by positive statements that a label is a NAME rather than a KEY, on both tiers. Ambiguity moves from authoring time - where refusing it is only a guess about intent - to LOOKUP time, where the candidates are in hand and a caller can choose. The lookup verb itself (address a pane by name or id; on 2+ matches fail and return the entries) is the requester's design and is deliberately a SEPARATE CR: it touches every pane-taking verb and needs an axi decision on how candidates are reported, which should not be invented as a rider on a tabs PR. Removing the rules is safe standalone - purely widening for apply, since every template legal today stays legal and behaves identically, and the only behavior change is that capture stops discarding labels.
